perturbation.original_text: 7.2. Franchisor shall be named as an additional insured on all such insurance policies and shall be provided with certificates of insurance evidencing such coverage. All public liability and property damage policies shall contain a provision that El Pollo Loco, although named as an insured, shall nevertheless be entitled to recover under such policies on any loss incurred by El Pollo Loco, its affiliates, agents and/or employees, by reason of the negligence of Developer, its principals, contractors, agents and/or employees. All policies shall provide Franchisor with at least thirty (30) days' notice of cancellation or termination of coverage.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2. Franchisor shall be named as an additional insured on all such insurance policies and shall be provided with certificates of insurance evidencing such coverage. All public liability and property damage policies shall contain a provision that El Pollo Loco, although named as an insured, shall nevertheless be entitled to recover under such policies on any loss incurred by El Pollo Loco, its affiliates, agents and/or employees, by reason of the negligence of Developer, its principals, contractors, agents and/or employees. All policies shall provide Franchisor with at least ten (10) days' notice of cancellation or termination of coverage.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for cancellation or termination of insurance coverage from 30 days to 10 days creates a contradiction, as many states require insurance companies to provide at least 30 days' notice to additional insured parties before canceling a policy. California, where the franchisor is located, is one such state.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 678 requires insurers to provide written notice to additional insured parties at least 30 days prior to the cancellation or nonrenewal of a policy. By shortening the notice period to 10 days, the modified text violates this California statute.

GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 11. Cancellation and Failure to Renew Certain Property Insurance [675 - 679.7] ( Chapter 11 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 313. ) 678. (a) (1) At least 45 days before the policy expiration, an insurer shall deliver to the named insured or mail to the named insured at the address shown in the policy, either of the following: (A) An offer of renewal of the policy contingent upon payment of premium as stated in the offer, stating each of the following: (i) Any reduction of limits or elimination of coverage. That reduction of limits or elimination of coverage shall identify the specific limits being reduced or coverage being eliminated by the offer of renewal. The elimination of coverage for the previously covered peril of fire shall be subject to subdivision (b) of Section 10103.6. (ii) The telephone number of the insurer’s representatives who handle consumer inquiries or complaints. The telephone number shall be displayed prominently in a font size consistent with the other text of the renewal offer. (B) A notice of nonrenewal of the policy. That notice shall
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the insurance cancellation notice period from 30 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation states that many states, including California where the franchisor is located, require at least 30 days' notice. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating California Insurance Code § 678 mandates a 30-day notice. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention the 30-day notice for additional insureds, it discusses notice periods for policy expiration (at least 45 days). The altered text directly contradicts the implied legal standard of a 30-day notice period for cancellation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 23.1. Developer acknowledges that it has received a complete copy of the El Pollo Loco® Franchise Disclosure Document, issuance date March 26, 2019 (Control No. 032619) at least fourteen (14) calendar days prior to the date on which this Agreement was executed by Developer or payment of any monies to the Franchisor.
perturbation.changed_text: 23.1. Developer acknowledges that it has received a complete copy of the El Pollo Loco® Franchise Disclosure Document, issuance date March 26, 2019 (Control No. 032619) at least five (5) calendar days prior to the date on which this Agreement was executed by Developer or payment of any monies to the Franchisor.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the FDD review period from 14 days to 5 days is a contradiction. Federal law requires a 14-day review period.
perturbation.law_explanation: 16 CFR § 436.5 mandates that a prospective franchisee receive the Franchise Disclosure Document at least 14 calendar days before signing a franchise agreement or paying any money to the franchisor. By reducing the review period to 5 days, the contract violates this federal regulation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 16 CFR § 436.5 - Disclosure items. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 16 CFR § 436.5 - Disclosure items. CFR prev | next § 436.5 Disclosure items. (a) Item 1 : The Franchisor, and any Parents, Predecessors, and Affiliates. Disclose: (1) The name and principal business address of the franchisor ; any parents; and any affiliates that offer franchises in any line of business or provide products or services to the franchisees of the franchisor . (2) The name and principal business address of any predecessors during the 10-year period immediately before the close of the franchisor 's most recent fiscal year . (3) The name that the franchisor uses and any names it intends to use to conduct business. (4) The identity and principal business address of the franchisor 's agent for service of process. (5) The type of business organization used by the franchisor (for example, corporation, partnership) and the state in which it was organized. (6) The following information about the franchisor 's business and the franchises offered: (i) Whether the franchisor operates businesses of the type being franchised. (ii) The franchisor 's other business activities. (iii) The business the franchisee will conduct. (iv) The general market for the product or service the franchisee will offer. In describing the general market, consider factors such as whether the market is developed or developing, whether the goods will be sold primarily to a certain group, and whether sales are seasonal. (v) In general terms, any laws or regulations specific to the industry in which the franchise business operates. (vi) A general description of the competition. (7) The prior business experience of the franchisor ; any predecessors listed in § 436.5(a)(2) of this part; and any affiliates that offer franchises in any line of business or provide products or services to the franchisees of the franchisor , including: (i)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the FDD review period from 14 days to 5 days, which directly contradicts the legal requirement stated in the Law Explanation that references 16 CFR § 436.5. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that federal law mandates a 14-day review period. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the 14-day requirement, the Law Explanation, which is based on 16 CFR § 436.5, states this requirement. Therefore, reducing the review period creates a clear non-compliance with the referenced regulation, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 21.1. For the purpose of this Agreement, all notices shall be in writing and shall be sent to the party to be charged with receipt thereof either (i) served personally, or (i) sent by certified or registered United States mail, or (ii) sent by reputable overnight delivery service, or (iv) sent by facsimile. Notices served personally are effective immediately on delivery, and those served by mail shall be deemed given forty-eight (48) hours after deposit of such notice in a United States post office with postage prepaid and duly addressed to the party to whom such notice or communication is directed. Notices served by overnight delivery shall be deemed to have been given the day after deposit of such notice with such service. Notices served via facsimile shall be deemed to have been given the day of faxing such notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 21.1. For the purpose of this Agreement, all notices shall be in writing and shall be sent to the party to be charged with receipt thereof either (i) served personally, or (i) sent by standard United States mail. Notices served personally are effective immediately on delivery, and those served by mail shall be deemed given immediately after deposit of such notice in a United States post office with postage prepaid and duly addressed to the party to whom such notice or communication is directed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice method to standard US mail and deeming it effective immediately upon deposit creates a legal contradiction. Standard mail delivery times are not guaranteed, and immediate effectiveness conflicts with the legal principle that parties must have reasonable notice. California law, where the franchisor is headquartered, requires reasonable notice for contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 19 states that 'Every person who has actual notice of circumstances sufficient to put a prudent man upon inquiry as to a particular fact, has constructive notice of the fact itself in all cases in which, by prosecuting such inquiry, he might have learned such fact.' This implies that notice should be reasonably discoverable. Immediate effectiveness upon mailing, with no regard to actual delivery time, is not reasonable and makes it so the Developer might not have learned such fact, and thus contradicts the intent of this code, especially if standard mail is used. While this doesn't specify a time frame, courts have interpreted 'reasonable notice' to mean a sufficient time to act on the notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 19 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS ( Preliminary Provisions enacted 1872. ) 19. Every person who has actual notice of circumstances sufficient to put a prudent person upon inquiry as to a particular fact has constructive notice of the fact itself in all cases in which, by prosecuting such inquiry, he or she might have learned that fact. (Amended by Stats. 2017, Ch. 561, Sec. 15. (AB 1516) Effective January 1, 2018.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notice provisions to allow for standard US mail with immediate effectiveness upon deposit. The perturbation explanation states this contradicts the legal principle that parties must have reasonable notice, aligning with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition. The Law Explanation cites California Civil Code § 19, stating that notice should be reasonably discoverable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 contains the text of California Civil Code § 19. Immediate effectiveness upon mailing, especially with standard mail, does not ensure reasonable discoverability, thus creating a contradiction by undermining the requirement for reasonable notice as implied by the code.
